title: Type-safe eval in Grace>
summary: The case for principled eval support
full_text:
This post is about a Grace programming language feature that is both cursed and blessed: type-safe eval. This feature is cursed because (like any eval) it can potentially promote untrusted input into code1, but also blessed because it's type-safe and it's the foundation for two other language features:

type-safe dynamic imports (very useful)

privacy-protecting prompt engineering (less useful, but nifty)

eval

What do I mean by type-safe eval? Well, I mean that the following Grace program is valid:
let increment x = import read "x + 1"

in increment 4 # Returns: 5

import read is the Grace version of eval: it converts a Text argument storing Grace code into a runnable expression you can use within the same Grace program. As Wikipedia puts it:

eval , short for evaluate, is a function which evaluates a string as though it were an expression in the language, and returns a result

This means that the previous Grace example behaves as if we had written:
let increment x = x + 1

in increment 4

Type safety

Grace's implementation of import read is type-safe, meaning that it does not violate Grace's type safety guarantees. Specifically, one of those type safety guarantees is that Grace never evaluates code containing type errors.

To see what I mean, consider this code:
let not x = import read "x == false"

in not 4

If you run that you'll get the following error message:
Not a subtype

The following type:

 Natural

(input):3:9: 
 │
3 │ in not 4
 │ ↑

… cannot be a subtype of:

 Bool

(read):1:6: 
 │
1 │ x == false
 │ ↑

… which essentially says that we can't compare 4 and false for equality. Moreover, type checking fails before the code inside of the Text is evaluated and the way Grace supports this is actually not that complicated but it's still something that very few languages have actually implemented2.

Grace's support for type-safe eval exploits the fact that the language is bidirectionally type-checked, meaning that the type checker can work backwards from how code is used to infer the types of inputs. For example, when the type checker processes the above expression it inserts placeholders into the code for unsolved types (e.g. a? and b? below):
let not (x : a?) = import read "x == false" : b?

in not 4 : b?

… and then as type-checking progresses it will refine these unsolved types as it gathers more information.

For example, when the type checker analyzes the not 4 at the end of the code it deduces that the previously unsolved type a? must be Natural (since we call the not function on 4, which is a Natural number).
let not (x : Natural) = import read "x == false" : b?

in not 4 : b?

… which gives us our "elaborated" expression at the end of the first type-checking step (yes, there is going to be another type-checking step in just a second). At this point, the type checker still doesn't know that there's a type error because we haven't done any evaluation yet (and therefore haven't evaled the Text).

Now we evaluate the code and before we evaluate the import read expression the interpreter runs a second type-checking step that type checks the code stored inside the Text. We know from the first type-checking step that the type of x has to be Natural (otherwise not 4 would be a type error) and then the type-checker notices that you can't compare a Natural number (x) with a Bool (false) for equality3, so the second type-checking step fails before the expression is evaled.

This is known as staged type-checking, meaning that the interpreter interleaves type-checking and evaluation appropriately whenever we eval code. Even as we interleave type-checking and evaluation we never evaluate code that hasn't been type-checked first.

Dynamic imports

At this point you're probably wondering: why does Grace even support type-safe eval?

Well, type-safe eval is actually a special case of a more general Grace feature which is type-safe dynamic imports. These dynamic imports can depend on values in scope when resolving the imported code.

import read is just one of many ways that Grace can dynamically import code:

you can dynamically import code from GitHub using import github

you can dynamically import code using an HTTP request using import http

you can dynamically generate code from an LLM using import prompt

you can dynamically read code from a string (Text) using import read

Dynamic imports are a feature that was requested over and over and over and over and over again for the last programming language I created (Dhall), and while Dhall never supported dynamic imports4 I was determined to support them in Grace.

To illustrate what I mean by a "dynamic import", consider this Grace code:
let prelude{ name } =
 import github
 { owner: "Gabriella439"
 , repository: "grace"
 , path: "prelude/${name}.ffg"
 }

in prelude

The above prelude function is a utility that imports code from Grace's prelude by the code's subpath within the GitHub repository. This import is dynamic because which code we import depends on a value (name) which we don't know yet.

That means when we pick a value for name, like this:
let prelude{ name } =
 import github
 { owner: "Gabriella439"
 , repository: "grace"
 , path: "prelude/${name}.ffg"
 }

let mystery = prelude{ name: "text/concat" }

in mystery [ "Hello, ", "world!" ] # Returns: "Hello, world!"

… the interpreter has to do type-checking in stages for this program to work (like before).

On the first type-checking pass the interpreter works backwards from how we use the mystery function and elaborates the code with these types:
let prelude{ name: Text } =
 import github
 { owner: "Gabriella439"
 , repository: "grace"
 , path: "prelude/${name}.ffg"
 } : List Text -> a?

let mystery : List Text -> a? = prelude{ name: "text/concat" }

in mystery [ "Hello, ", "world!" ] : a?

After that first type-checking pass the interpreter then evaluates the expression and the import github expression returns the following code:
# Concatenate a `List` of `Text`
let concat
 : List Text -> Text
 = fold { cons: \x y -> (x + y) : Text, nil: "" }

in concat

Then the interpreter performs a second type-checking pass on that code and checks that the inferred type (List Text -> Text) matches mystery's expected type (List Text -> a?). Those two types can only match if we replace all occurrences of the unsolved type a? with Text, which gives us the final elaborated program after the second type-checking pass:
let mystery : List Text -> Text =
 ( let concat
 : List Text -> Text
 = fold { cons: \x y -> (x + y) : Text, nil: "" }

 in concat
 )

in mystery [ "Hello, ", "world!" ] : Text

… and then evaluation successfully proceeds from there.

Privacy-protecting prompt engineering

A common prompt engineering concern is feeding sensitive or proprietary data into a large language model. For example, at my job (Mercury), we avoid feeding sensitive customer data into a large language model even though providers like OpenAI provide data privacy guarantees for enterprise accounts.

Grace provides a neat (albeit very limited) feature where a large language model can operate on sensitive data without the data actually being fed to the model (in fact, the sensitive data never leaves the interpreter). This feature is more of a novelty but I thought I would mention it to give people ideas.

I'll illustrate how this works by starting off with the following contrived example of what we're trying to AVOID:
let private = { firstName: "Mary", lastName: "Sue" }

in prompt
 { key: ./openai.key
 , text:
 "Combine ${private.firstName} and ${private.lastName}"
 } : { fullName: Text } # Returns: { fullName: "Mary Sue" }

Anything we stuff in the prompt there will be sent over the network to OpenAI. However, we can change the above program to do the same thing in a privacy-protecting way by making two small changes:
let private = { firstName: "Mary", lastName: "Sue" }

in import prompt
 { key: ./openai.key
 , text:
 "Combine private.firstName and private.lastName"
 } : { fullName: Text } # Same result

Can you spot the difference? The two changes are:

we prefix the prompt keyword with import

By itself, the prompt keyword can only return JSON (not arbitrary code) and also doesn't have access to values in scope. With import the prompt keyword can return arbitrary Grace code and that Grace code can refer to values in scope5.

we no longer interpolate private.firstName and private.lastName into the prompt

The string that the LLM sees is literally:

Combine private.firstName and private.lastName

So wait … how does this even work? How does the LLM return the correct answer if we're no longer putting the sensitive data in the prompt?

Well, I lied a little bit; the actual prompt the LLM sees is (essentially) this:

The following values are in scope:
private : { firstName: Text, lastName: Text }

Using those values in scope, write a Grace program that produces a value of this type:
{ fullName: Text }

… by following these instructions:

Combine private.firstName and private.lastName

… and then the LLM sees those instructions and responds with something like this Grace code:
"${private.firstName} ${private.lastName}"

… and then Grace's support for type-safe eval takes it from there. That code is checked for type safety and then injected back into the program as if we had written:
let private = { firstName: "Mary", lastName: "Sue" }

in "${private.firstName} ${private.lastName}"

… which still produces the final (correct) result.

Throughout this process the LLM never sees the data. The LLM only sees the shape of the data (it knows that firstName and lastName are both Text) and the LLM responds with instructions for what to do with the data (in the form of Grace code) but it's the Grace interpreter that executes those instructions, not the LLM.

This means that there are limits to how much you can do with this trick to protect sensitive data. The LLM can only transform the data using Grace code so it can't do anything to the data that Grace wasn't already able to do6.

Conclusion

The coolest part is that all of these features are implemented in a typed programming language with type inference. If you were to ask most programmers how they'd implement these features they'd probably assume that you would need to do this sort of cursed thing in an untyped language, but they'd be wrong! All of these features work in a typed language if you're willing to put in the engineering work.

I push pretty hard on making these features work with types because I don't believe that stack traces are a suitable substitute for type checking and talk about this in more detail in another post of mine: Dynamic type errors lack relevance.

If you're interested in learning more about Grace, you can visit the GitHub repository for Grace or try Grace out in your browser.
